  "opinion_text": "As a man who has spent his life in Naples, working retail and baking as a hobby, I've seen how people can be held back by expectations that aren't fair. Stereotype threat is like when someone believes others expect them to fail because of their background, even if they have the skills to succeed. In STEM fields—science, technology, engineering, and math—this can happen to women or people from certain ethnic groups who are expected to not do as well. It's a kind of pressure that makes them doubt themselves, even when they shouldn't.\n\nI believe we should address this because no one deserves to be held back by unfair expectations. As a Christian, I believe everyone is created equal and has potential. If we all try to see people for who they are, not just their background or gender, maybe this threat would disappear. It won't happen overnight, but like baking a good loaf of bread, it takes time and care. Let's start by listening more and judging less."

response_parsed.opinion_text:
As a man who has spent his life in Naples, working retail and baking as a hobby, I've seen how people can be held back by expectations that aren't fair. Stereotype threat is like when someone believes others expect them to fail because of their background, even if they have the skills to succeed. In STEM fields—science, technology, engineering, and math—this can happen to women or people from certain ethnic groups who are expected to not do as well. It's a kind of pressure that makes them doubt themselves, even when they shouldn't.

I believe we should address this because no one deserves to be held back by unfair expectations. Imagine if my grandson, who loves building things like I love baking, felt he couldn't become an engineer because people doubted him. That would be a shame, wouldn't it? We need to make sure everyone has the chance to succeed based on their own abilities.

How can we do this? Well, for one, we could create environments where people feel supported and valued, no matter their background. Schools and workplaces should encourage teamwork, so that people from different backgrounds learn to trust each other. We could also make sure that role models—people who look like my grandson or the young women I see in the market—are visible in STEM fields, showing that anyone can succeed with hard work.

We also need to be mindful of our own expectations. As a Christian, I believe everyone is created equal and has potential. If we all try to see people for who they are, not just their background or gender, maybe this threat would disappear. It won't happen overnight, but like baking a good loaf of bread, it takes time and care. Let's start by listening more and judging less.
